params.text:
Capacity, consent and the Mental Capacity Act

Care and support depend on a person's agreement. The Mental Capacity Act 2005 sets out how to support people to make their own decisions and what to do when someone may not be able to. It applies to everyone aged 16 and over in England and Wales.

  • Assume capacity: start from the belief that a person can make their own decisions.
  • Support the decision: give information in a way the person can understand before deciding they cannot choose.
  • Unwise is allowed: a person has the right to make what others see as an unwise choice.

Capacity is decision-specific — a person may manage some decisions and need help with others.

When someone cannot decide

Where a person genuinely cannot make a particular decision, even with support, the Mental Capacity Act requires that any decision made on their behalf is in their best interests and is the least restrictive option available. Formal capacity assessments and best-interests decisions are led by senior staff or professionals, not by you alone.

Your role is still important. You gain consent for the everyday care you give — explaining what you would like to do and checking the person agrees — and you watch for signs that someone's ability to decide may be changing. Consent can be withdrawn at any moment, and you respect that. If you are unsure whether a person understands a choice, or you notice a change in their capacity, report it so it can be looked at properly. Never simply assume that someone cannot decide.

params.question: A person makes a choice about their care that you think is unwise but they clearly understand it. What does the Mental Capacity Act expect?

answers.text: Respect their decision, as a person with capacity has the right to make an unwise choice

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The Act protects a person's right to make their own decisions, including ones others see as unwise.

answers.text: Overrule them, because your job is to keep them safe

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Making an unwise choice does not, on its own, mean a person lacks capacity to decide.

answers.text: Assume they lack capacity because the choice seems unwise

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. An unwise decision is not proof of lacking capacity; you must still assume capacity.

answers.text: Ask a relative to make the decision for them instead

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. A relative cannot simply take over a decision a person with capacity is able to make.
